brief_document.title: Cron expression parsing and next-fire-time calculation
brief_document.purpose: Integrate a cron expression parser and implement next-fire-time calculation for both cron and interval triggers. This is the time-math foundation the ScheduleEvaluator uses to determine when each schedule should fire next.
brief_document.task: Add a cron parsing dependency (saffron or cron crate) to the aion crate. Implement a next_fire_time function that, given a TriggerSpec and a reference DateTime, returns the next DateTime the schedule should fire. For Cron, use the parser to compute the next matching time. For Interval, add the period to the reference time. Handle edge cases: invalid cron expressions return a typed error, intervals of zero duration are rejected.

requirements.title: Cron expression parsing with validated input
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL parse standard 5-field cron expressions (minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week) and reject invalid expressions with a typed error. Extended 6-field (with seconds) SHOULD be supported if the chosen crate supports it.
acceptance: Empty string returns a typed parse error.

requirements.title: Next-fire-time calculation for cron and interval triggers
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL implement next_fire_time(trigger: &TriggerSpec, after: DateTime<Utc>) -> Result<DateTime<Utc>, ScheduleError> that returns the next fire time strictly after the given reference time. For Cron triggers, this uses the parsed expression. For Interval triggers, this adds the period to the reference time.
acceptance: A cron expression "0 0 * * *" with reference 2026-06-07T00:00:00Z returns 2026-06-08T00:00:00Z.
An interval of 5 minutes with reference T returns T + 5 minutes.
An interval of zero duration returns an error.
Multiple successive calls return strictly increasing times.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement the ScheduleEvaluator loop — that is AK-003.
SHALL NOT integrate with the timer wheel — that is AK-003.
SHALL NOT add overlap or catch-up policy logic — that is AK-003.
verification: Unit tests cover standard cron expressions, intervals, edge cases, and error paths.

constraints.text: Schedule state is projected from events, never stored as mutable fields.
Schedule evaluation uses durable timers (AT infrastructure), not wall-clock polling.
Overlap and catch-up policies are explicit per-schedule configuration, not implicit engine behavior.
Recovery must reconstruct schedule state from events and re-arm timers for all active schedules.
A deleted schedule's events are retained for audit but the schedule is no longer evaluated.
Schedule mutations are serialized per ScheduleId to prevent concurrent modification races.

resolved_context.intention: Production workflow engines must support time-triggered execution: run this billing workflow every midnight, check this API every 5 minutes, generate this report every Monday at 9am. Without built-in scheduling, every user must build their own cron-to-workflow bridge, duplicating coordination logic (overlap prevention, catch-up, pause/resume) that belongs in the engine. This cluster adds a Schedule resource to Aion: a persistent, durable configuration that starts workflow executions on a cron expression or fixed interval, with policies for overlap, catch-up on missed triggers, and pause/resume. Schedules are engine-level — they survive restarts, are queryable, and their trigger history is auditable.
